ICC Cricket World Cup 2003 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

======================================================================

Tournament: 2003 ICC Cricket World Cup
Host Countries: South Africa, Zimbabwe, Kenya
Dates: 9 February – 23 March 2003
Teams: 14 (Australia, Bangladesh, Canada, England, India, Kenya, Namibia, Netherlands, New Zealand, Pakistan, South Africa, Sri Lanka, West Indies, Zimbabwe)
Winner: Australia (defeated India in the Final)
Runner-up: India
Player of the Tournament: Sachin Tendulkar
Top Run Scorer: Sachin Tendulkar (673 runs in 11 matches)
Top Wicket Taker: Chaminda Vaas (23 wickets)

metadata.section_title: ICC Cricket World Cup 2003 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

chunks.text:
(defeated India in the Final)
Runner-up: India
Player of the Tournament: Sachin Tendulkar
Top Run Scorer: Sachin Tendulkar (673 runs in 11 matches)
Top Wicket Taker: Chaminda Vaas (23 wickets)

=== TOP MEMORABLE MOMENTS ===

1. Sachin Tendulkar's 673-Run Campaign:
   Sachin Tendulkar scored 673 runs in 11 innings at an average of 61.18. He hit one century (98 vs Pakistan was agonizingly close). He scored 152 against Namibia, his highest World Cup score. Tendulkar won the Player of the Tournament award. He was the tournament's leading run scorer by a significant margin.

2. Australia's Unbeaten Run — 11 Wins in 11 Matches:
   Australia won every single match in the 2003 World Cup, going 11-0 through the tournament. This was the first time a team went unbeaten through an entire World Cup. Ricky Ponting led the team with ruthless efficiency. Their winning streak eventually extended to a record-breaking 34 consecutive World Cup wins across multiple tournaments.

an entire World Cup. Ricky Ponting led the team with ruthless efficiency. Their winning streak eventually extended to a record-breaking 34 consecutive World Cup wins across multiple tournaments.

3. The 2003 Final — Australia 359/2 vs India 234 all out:
   Date: 23 March 2003. Venue: Wanderers Stadium, Johannesburg.
   Australia batted first and posted 359/2 in 50 overs — the highest score in a World Cup final at that time.
   Ricky Ponting scored a magnificent 140* off 121 balls (4x4s: 4, 8x6s: 4) in one of the greatest World Cup final innings ever.
   Damien Martyn contributed 88* off 84 balls in an unbeaten 234-run partnership with Ponting for the 3rd wicket.
   India were bowled out for 234 in 39.2 overs. Virender Sehwag scored 82 off 81 balls but India collapsed after his dismissal.
   Sachin Tendulkar was dismissed early for 4 runs, caught by Darren Lehmann off Glenn McGrath's bowling.
   Glenn McGrath took 3/52 and was named Player of the Match.
   Australia won by 125 runs, claiming their 3rd World Cup title.
   Turning Point: Tendulkar's early dismissal removed India's biggest threat and demoralized the team.

named Player of the Match.
   Australia won by 125 runs, claiming their 3rd World Cup title.
   Turning Point: Tendulkar's early dismissal removed India's biggest threat and demoralized the team.

4. India vs Pakistan — The High-Voltage Clash:
   Date: 1 March 2003. Venue: Centurion, Pretoria.
   India beat Pakistan by 6 wickets in the group stage. Sachin Tendulkar scored 98 off 75 balls, dominating Shoaib Akhtar and Wasim Akram.
   Tendulkar famously upper-cut Shoaib Akhtar for six over third man — one of the most iconic shots in World Cup history.
   India chased down Pakistan's 273/7 with ease, reaching 276/4 in 45.4 overs.

5. Kenya's Fairy-Tale Run to Semi-Finals:
   Kenya became the first non-Test nation to reach a World Cup semi-final.
   They beat Sri Lanka by 53 runs in the group stage, which was a massive upset.
   They received walkovers from New Zealand and Zimbabwe (who refused to play in Kenya for safety/political reasons).
   Kenya eventually lost to India in the semi-final.
   Collins Obuya's leg-spin (5/24 vs Sri Lanka) was one of the performances of the tournament.

to play in Kenya for safety/political reasons).
   Kenya eventually lost to India in the semi-final.
   Collins Obuya's leg-spin (5/24 vs Sri Lanka) was one of the performances of the tournament.

6. South Africa's Tragic Exit — The Rain Rule Confusion:
   In the group match vs Sri Lanka at Durban on 3 March 2003, South Africa needed to win to advance.
   Chasing Sri Lanka's 268/9, South Africa's Mark Boucher blocked the last ball with the score at 229/6, not realizing they needed to score one more run.
   Rain had reduced the target via DLS method, and South Africa tied the match when they needed to win.
   Captain Shaun Pollock was devastated. South Africa were eliminated from their home World Cup in heartbreaking fashion.
   This is one of the most painful moments in World Cup history — a host nation eliminated by a mathematical miscalculation.

were eliminated from their home World Cup in heartbreaking fashion.
   This is one of the most painful moments in World Cup history — a host nation eliminated by a mathematical miscalculation.

7. Chaminda Vaas's Hat-Trick — First Ball, Second Ball, Third Ball:
   Date: 19 February 2003. Match: Sri Lanka vs Bangladesh at Pietermaritzburg.
   Chaminda Vaas took a hat-trick with the first three balls of the match — the first hat-trick to start a World Cup match.
   He finished with figures of 6/25 in 9.1 overs, the best bowling figures in the 2003 World Cup.
   Bangladesh were bowled out for just 124. Sri Lanka won by 10 wickets.

8. Andy Bichel's All-Round Heroics:
   Andy Bichel produced one of the greatest all-round performances in World Cup history against England.
   He took 7/20 with the ball (the best bowling figures in the 2003 World Cup after Vaas's 6/25) to bowl England out for 204.
   Then he scored 34* with the bat to guide Australia to victory, hitting the winning runs.

the ball (the best bowling figures in the 2003 World Cup after Vaas's 6/25) to bowl England out for 204.
   Then he scored 34* with the bat to guide Australia to victory, hitting the winning runs.

9. Shoaib Akhtar's Raw Pace:
   Shoaib Akhtar bowled the fastest delivery ever recorded in international cricket during the 2003 World Cup — 161.3 km/h (100.2 mph) against England.
   This record stood for many years and remains one of the fastest balls ever bowled.

10. Canada vs Bangladesh — The Associate Upset:
    Date: 11 February 2003. John Davison of Canada scored the fastest century in World Cup history at that time — 67 balls.
    Canada posted 180/7 but Bangladesh chased it down with 3 wickets to spare.

=== RECORDS SET IN 2003 WORLD CUP ===

- Highest team score in a World Cup final: Australia 359/2 vs India (Wanderers, Johannesburg)
- Ricky Ponting 140* — highest individual score in a World Cup final at that time
- Australia's 11-0 unbeaten run through the entire tournament
- Chaminda Vaas hat-trick off first three balls of a match (vs Bangladesh)
- Shoaib Akhtar's 161.3 km/h — fastest delivery in international cricket
- Sachin Tendulkar — 673 runs, Player of the Tournament
- Glenn McGrath 3/52 in the final — key to Australia's dominance
- John Davison (Canada) — 67-ball century, fastest in World Cup at that time
- Andy Bichel 7/20 vs England — second-best bowling figures in 2003 WC

3/52 in the final — key to Australia's dominance
- John Davison (Canada) — 67-ball century, fastest in World Cup at that time
- Andy Bichel 7/20 vs England — second-best bowling figures in 2003 WC

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

1. Kenya beat Sri Lanka by 53 runs (Group B, 24 Feb 2003)

2. Kenya beat Zimbabwe by 7 wickets (Super Six, 12 March 2003)

3. Zimbabwe beat India by 2 wickets (Group A, 12 Feb 2003) — a rare WC loss for India against an associate/non-Test nation

4. Bangladesh beat Canada (11 Feb 2003)

=== TOURNAMENT FORMAT 2003 ===

Group Stage: 14 teams divided into 2 groups of 7 (Group A and Group B). Each team played every other team in their group once. Top 3 from each group advanced to the Super Six stage.
Super Six: 6 teams played only the 3 teams from the other group (carried forward results against teams from their own group that also qualified). Top 4 advanced to semi-finals.
Semi-Finals: Two semi-finals decided the finalists.
Final: Held at the Wanderers Stadium, Johannesburg on 23 March 2003.
Points: 2 for a win, 1 for a tie/no-result, 0 for a loss.

=== KEY UNUSUAL EVENTS ===

- England and New Zealand refused to play their scheduled matches in Zimbabwe and Kenya respectively, citing safety concerns. They forfeited those matches.
- Shane Warne was sent home before the tournament started after testing positive for a banned diuretic substance.
- Bob Woolmer served as Pakistan's coach during the tournament.
- The tournament was the last World Cup for several legends including Wasim Akram.

ICC Cricket World Cup 2007 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

======================================================================

Tournament: 2007 ICC Cricket World Cup
Host Country: West Indies (various Caribbean islands)
Dates: 13 March – 28 April 2007
Teams: 16 (Australia, Bangladesh, Bermuda, Canada, England, India, Ireland, Kenya, Netherlands, New Zealand, Pakistan, Scotland, South Africa, Sri Lanka, West Indies, Zimbabwe)
Winner: Australia (defeated Sri Lanka in the Final)
Runner-up: Sri Lanka
Player of the Tournament: Glenn McGrath
Top Run Scorer: Matthew Hayden (659 runs)
Top Wicket Taker: Glenn McGrath (26 wickets)

metadata.section_title: ICC Cricket World Cup 2007 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

chunks.text:
Australia (defeated Sri Lanka in the Final)
Runner-up: Sri Lanka
Player of the Tournament: Glenn McGrath
Top Run Scorer: Matthew Hayden (659 runs)
Top Wicket Taker: Glenn McGrath (26 wickets)

=== TOP MEMORABLE MOMENTS ===

1. The Biggest Upset in World Cup History — Ireland Beat Pakistan:
   Date: 17 March 2007. Venue: Sabina Park, Kingston, Jamaica.
   Ireland beat Pakistan by 3 wickets in one of the greatest upsets in cricket history.
   Pakistan batted first and scored 132 all out. Boyd Rankin took 3/32.
   Ireland chased it down reaching 133/7 with Niall O'Brien scoring 72.
   This result, combined with Pakistan's loss to West Indies, eliminated Pakistan from the group stage.
   This was the match after which Pakistan coach Bob Woolmer was found dead in his hotel room on 18 March 2007 — one of the most shocking and tragic events in cricket history.

metadata.section_title: Australia (defeated Sri Lanka in the Final)

chunks.text:
from the group stage.
   This was the match after which Pakistan coach Bob Woolmer was found dead in his hotel room on 18 March 2007 — one of the most shocking and tragic events in cricket history.

2. Bob Woolmer's Death:
   Pakistan coach Bob Woolmer was found unconscious in his hotel room in Kingston, Jamaica on 18 March 2007, the day after Pakistan's elimination from the World Cup.
   He was pronounced dead at the hospital. Initial reports suggested he was murdered (manual strangulation).
   A Jamaican police investigation followed, but in June 2007, the verdict was changed to death by natural causes (heart failure).
   This remains one of the most tragic and controversial events in World Cup history.

3. Bangladesh Beat India in the Group Stage:
   Date: 17 March 2007. Venue: Queen's Park Oval, Port of Spain.
   Bangladesh beat India by 5 wickets in a massive upset.
   India scored 191 all out. Mashrafe Mortaza took 4/38.
   Bangladesh chased it down with 5 wickets in hand, with Mushfiqur Rahim and Tamim Iqbal contributing.
   Combined with India's loss to Sri Lanka, India were eliminated in the group stage — a humiliating exit for one of cricket's biggest teams.
   This was India's earliest exit from a World Cup since 1999.

with India's loss to Sri Lanka, India were eliminated in the group stage — a humiliating exit for one of cricket's biggest teams.
   This was India's earliest exit from a World Cup since 1999.

4. India's Group Stage Elimination:
   India failed to advance past the group stage, losing to Bangladesh and Sri Lanka.
   Captain Rahul Dravid faced massive criticism. The team returned to effigy-burning protests across India.
   This World Cup exit led to major changes in Indian cricket management and coaching.

5. Australia's Third Consecutive World Cup Title:
   Australia won the 2007 World Cup, their 3rd consecutive title (1999, 2003, 2007) and 4th overall.
   They went unbeaten again, winning all 11 matches.
   This extended their unbeaten World Cup streak to 29 consecutive wins (2003 full tournament + 2007 full tournament).
   Adam Gilchrist's stunning century in the final sealed their dominance.

This extended their unbeaten World Cup streak to 29 consecutive wins (2003 full tournament + 2007 full tournament).
   Adam Gilchrist's stunning century in the final sealed their dominance.

6. The 2007 Final — Australia 281/4 vs Sri Lanka 215/8:
   Date: 28 April 2007. Venue: Kensington Oval, Bridgetown, Barbados.
   The final started under lights but was interrupted by rain and bad light.
   Adam Gilchrist scored 149 off 104 balls — one of the greatest World Cup final innings ever.
   He used a squash ball inside his left glove for better grip — an unusual technique revelation.
   Australia posted 281/4. Sri Lanka were restricted to 215/8 (36 overs, rain-revised target 269 in 36 overs).
   Glenn McGrath, in his final international match, took 0/14 in 4 overs and was named Player of the Tournament.
   Player of the Match: Adam Gilchrist.
   Turning Point: Gilchrist's explosive batting destroyed Sri Lanka's bowling attack in the first 20 overs.

in 4 overs and was named Player of the Tournament.
   Player of the Match: Adam Gilchrist.
   Turning Point: Gilchrist's explosive batting destroyed Sri Lanka's bowling attack in the first 20 overs.

7. Glenn McGrath's Farewell:
   The 2007 World Cup final was Glenn McGrath's last international match.
   He finished with 26 wickets in the tournament — the top wicket-taker.
   He won the Player of the Tournament award — a fitting farewell to one of the greatest fast bowlers ever.
   Across World Cups 2003 and 2007, McGrath was virtually unplayable.

8. The Super Eight Controversy:
   The 2007 format used a Super Eight stage where 8 teams played each other.
   Points from the group stage were carried forward — only points from matches against other Super Eight qualifiers.
   This format was criticized for being too long and making early upsets less meaningful.

from the group stage were carried forward — only points from matches against other Super Eight qualifiers.
   This format was criticized for being too long and making early upsets less meaningful.

9. Herschelle Gibbs's Six Sixes:
   Herschelle Gibbs of South Africa hit six sixes in one over bowled by Daan van Bunge of the Netherlands.
   Date: 16 March 2007. Venue: Warner Park, Basseterre, St Kitts.
   Gibbs became only the third player in international cricket to achieve this feat.
   South Africa scored 353/3, with Gibbs making 72 off 40 balls.

10. Bermuda's World Cup Appearance:
    Bermuda qualified for their only World Cup in 2007.
    They lost all 3 group matches but the experience was historic for the tiny island nation.
    Their match against India featured some viral moments including their larger-bodied players.

=== RECORDS SET IN 2007 WORLD CUP ===

- Adam Gilchrist 149 off 104 balls in the World Cup final — highest score in a WC final
- Australia's 3rd consecutive World Cup title (1999, 2003, 2007)
- Herschelle Gibbs — 6 sixes in an over vs Netherlands
- Glenn McGrath — 26 wickets, Player of the Tournament (his farewell tournament)
- Matthew Hayden — 659 runs (tournament top scorer)
- Ireland beating Pakistan — one of the biggest upsets in WC history
- Australia unbeaten run extended to 29 consecutive WC wins

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

1. Ireland beat Pakistan by 3 wickets (Group D, 17 March 2007) — led to Pakistan's elimination

upsets in WC history
- Australia unbeaten run extended to 29 consecutive WC wins

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

1. Ireland beat Pakistan by 3 wickets (Group D, 17 March 2007) — led to Pakistan's elimination

2. Bangladesh beat India by 5 wickets (Group B, 17 March 2007) — contributed to India's elimination

3. India eliminated in group stage for the first time since 1999

4. Pakistan eliminated in group stage after losing to Ireland and West Indies

=== TOURNAMENT FORMAT 2007 ===

Group Stage: 16 teams divided into 4 groups of 4. Each team played every other team in their group once. Top 2 from each group advanced to the Super Eight.
Super Eight: 8 teams played each of the teams from other groups (carried forward results against other qualifiers from own group). Top 4 advanced to semi-finals.
Semi-Finals: Two semi-finals.
Final: Kensington Oval, Bridgetown, Barbados on 28 April 2007.
Points: 2 for a win, 1 for tie/no-result, 0 for a loss.
Points Carry Forward Rule: In the Super Eight stage, teams carried forward the points earned in group matches ONLY against teams that also qualified for the Super Eight from the same group.

=== KEY UNUSUAL EVENTS ===

- Bob Woolmer's death in Jamaica overshadowed the tournament
- Rain disrupted the final, requiring a revised DLS target
- The tournament lasted 47 days — the longest World Cup ever, drawing criticism
- Fans in India burned effigies of players after the team's early exit
- Australia were so dominant that the tournament lacked competitive tension in knockout stages

ICC Cricket World Cup 2011 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

======================================================================

Tournament: 2011 ICC Cricket World Cup
Host Countries: India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh
Dates: 19 February – 2 April 2011
Teams: 14 (Australia, Bangladesh, Canada, England, India, Ireland, Kenya, Netherlands, New Zealand, Pakistan, South Africa, Sri Lanka, West Indies, Zimbabwe)
Winner: India (defeated Sri Lanka in the Final)
Runner-up: Sri Lanka
Player of the Tournament: Yuvraj Singh
Top Run Scorer: Tillakaratne Dilshan (497 runs)
Top Wicket Taker: Zaheer Khan (21 wickets)

metadata.section_title: ICC Cricket World Cup 2011 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

chunks.text:
India (defeated Sri Lanka in the Final)
Runner-up: Sri Lanka
Player of the Tournament: Yuvraj Singh
Top Run Scorer: Tillakaratne Dilshan (497 runs)
Top Wicket Taker: Zaheer Khan (21 wickets)

=== TOP MEMORABLE MOMENTS ===

1. India Wins the World Cup After 28 Years:
   India won the 2011 World Cup, their second ODI World Cup title (first since 1983).
   It was the first time a host nation won the World Cup since Australia co-hosted and won in 1992.
   MS Dhoni finished the match with a massive six over long-on — one of the most iconic moments in cricket history.
   The win was especially emotional as it was Sachin Tendulkar's last World Cup — his teammates carried him on their shoulders during the victory lap.

2. The 2011 Final — India 277/4 vs Sri Lanka 274/6:
   Date: 2 April 2011. Venue: Wankhede Stadium, Mumbai.
   Sri Lanka batted first and scored 274/6 in 50 overs.
   Mahela Jayawardene scored a sublime 103* off 88 balls — one of the finest World Cup final innings ever.
   India's chase started badly: Virender Sehwag was out for a duck off the second ball, and Sachin Tendulkar was dismissed for 18.
   India were 31/2 when Gautam Gambhir (97 off 122 balls) and MS Dhoni (91* off 79 balls) rebuilt the innings.
   Gambhir's selfless 97 was arguably the most underrated innings of the final.
   MS Dhoni promoted himself above Yuvraj Singh at No. 5 — a bold captaincy decision that proved decisive.
   Dhoni finished the match with a six off Nuwan Kulasekara to seal the victory. India won by 6 wickets with 10 balls to spare.
   Player of the Match: MS Dhoni.
   Turning Points: (a) Dhoni's decision to bat at No. 5 ahead of Yuvraj, (b) Gambhir's partnership with Dhoni that stabilized the chase after 2 early wickets.

metadata.section_title: India (defeated Sri Lanka in the Final)

chunks.text:
Player of the Match: MS Dhoni.
   Turning Points: (a) Dhoni's decision to bat at No. 5 ahead of Yuvraj, (b) Gambhir's partnership with Dhoni that stabilized the chase after 2 early wickets.

3. Yuvraj Singh's Magnificent All-Round Tournament:
   Yuvraj Singh was named Player of the Tournament.
   He scored 362 runs (average 90.50) and took 15 wickets (average 25.13) — a rare all-round double.
   He scored 4 fifties and took crucial wickets in every knockout match.
   Yuvraj was later diagnosed with a rare germ cell cancer between his lungs and was treated in the US. His World Cup performance became even more remarkable in hindsight.

4. Kevin O'Brien's Fastest World Cup Century — Ireland vs England:
   Date: 2 March 2011. Venue: M Chinnaswamy Stadium, Bangalore.
   Ireland chased down England's 327/8 thanks to Kevin O'Brien's extraordinary 113 off just 63 balls.
   This was the fastest century in World Cup history at that time.
   O'Brien hit 13 fours and 6 sixes. Ireland won by 3 wickets.
   England were humiliated in one of the biggest upsets of the tournament.
   This remains the highest successful run chase by an associate/affiliate nation in World Cup history.

won by 3 wickets.
   England were humiliated in one of the biggest upsets of the tournament.
   This remains the highest successful run chase by an associate/affiliate nation in World Cup history.

5. India vs Pakistan Semi-Final — The Most Watched Cricket Match Ever:
   Date: 30 March 2011. Venue: Punjab Cricket Association Stadium, Mohali.
   An estimated TV audience of over 1 billion people watched this match.
   India batted first and scored 260/9. Sachin Tendulkar scored 85 and was dropped multiple times by Pakistan fielders (notably Misbah-ul-Haq's side).
   Pakistan were bowled out for 231. Wahab Riaz took 5/46 for Pakistan but it wasn't enough.
   The atmosphere was electric — even Indian Prime Minister Manmohan Singh and Pakistan PM Yousuf Raza Gilani attended together in a famous "cricket diplomacy" moment.
   Player of the Match: Sachin Tendulkar.

was electric — even Indian Prime Minister Manmohan Singh and Pakistan PM Yousuf Raza Gilani attended together in a famous "cricket diplomacy" moment.
   Player of the Match: Sachin Tendulkar.

6. Sachin Tendulkar's 2011 World Cup — His Last Dance:
   Tendulkar scored 482 runs in 9 innings at an average of 53.55.
   He scored 2 centuries (120 vs England, 111 vs South Africa).
   After the final win, he famously said: "Tendulkar has carried the weight of the nation on his shoulders for 22 years. It is time we carried him."
   His teammates literally carried him on a victory lap of the Wankhede Stadium.

7. South Africa's Semi-Final Heartbreak (Again):
   South Africa were knocked out in the quarter-finals by New Zealand.
   New Zealand chased down 222 with 6 wickets and 13 balls to spare.
   South Africa's "chokers" label in World Cup knockout matches continued.

were knocked out in the quarter-finals by New Zealand.
   New Zealand chased down 222 with 6 wickets and 13 balls to spare.
   South Africa's "chokers" label in World Cup knockout matches continued.

8. Virender Sehwag's 175 vs Bangladesh:
   In the opening match of the tournament, Sehwag scored 175 off 140 balls against Bangladesh in Dhaka.
   This was the highest individual score in the 2011 World Cup.
   India won by 87 runs to start their campaign strongly.

9. Strauss Hits Century in Vain as England Collapse:
   Andrew Strauss scored 158 against India, but England still lost by a tight margin in a high-scoring group match.
   The match at Bangalore saw 700+ runs scored.

10. Lasith Malinga's Bowling Masterclass:
    Malinga took 6/38 vs Kenya — the best bowling figures of the 2011 World Cup.
    He was Sri Lanka's strike bowler throughout and took several crucial wickets in knockout stages.

Masterclass:
    Malinga took 6/38 vs Kenya — the best bowling figures of the 2011 World Cup.
    He was Sri Lanka's strike bowler throughout and took several crucial wickets in knockout stages.

=== RECORDS SET IN 2011 WORLD CUP ===

- Kevin O'Brien 113 off 63 balls — fastest century in World Cup history (at that time)
- Ireland's 329/7 chasing 328 — highest successful chase by an associate nation in World Cup history
- Virender Sehwag 175 — highest individual score in 2011 WC
- Yuvraj Singh — Player of Tournament with 362 runs and 15 wickets (rare double)
- India became the first host nation to win a World Cup since Australia in 1992
- MS Dhoni's winning six in the final became an iconic moment
- Over 1 billion TV viewers for India vs Pakistan semi-final
- Mahela Jayawardene 103* in the final — 4th century in a World Cup final
- Zaheer Khan 21 wickets — leading wicket-taker of the tournament

Over 1 billion TV viewers for India vs Pakistan semi-final
- Mahela Jayawardene 103* in the final — 4th century in a World Cup final
- Zaheer Khan 21 wickets — leading wicket-taker of the tournament

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

1. Ireland beat England by 3 wickets (chasing 328, Group B, 2 March 2011) — Kevin O'Brien's 63-ball century

2. Bangladesh beat Ireland by 27 runs (Group B)

3. Kenya never won a match in the tournament

4. Netherlands beat England isn't correct for 2011; but Ireland's chase of 328 was the shock result

=== TOURNAMENT FORMAT 2011 ===

Group Stage: 14 teams divided into 2 groups of 7 (Group A and Group B). Each team played every other team in their group once. Top 4 from each group advanced to the quarter-finals.
Quarter-Finals: 8 teams. A1 vs B4, A2 vs B3, A3 vs B2, A4 vs B1.
Semi-Finals: Winners of quarter-finals. SF1: QF1 winner vs QF2 winner. SF2: QF3 winner vs QF4 winner.
Final: Wankhede Stadium, Mumbai on 2 April 2011.
Points: 2 for a win, 1 for tie/no-result, 0 for loss.

Quarter-Final Results:
- Pakistan beat West Indies by 10 wickets
- India beat Australia by 5 wickets
- Sri Lanka beat England by 10 wickets
- New Zealand beat South Africa by 49 runs

Semi-Final Results:
- India beat Pakistan by 29 runs (Mohali)
- Sri Lanka beat New Zealand by 5 wickets (Colombo)

metadata.section_title: Over 1 billion TV viewers for India vs Pakistan semi-final

chunks.text:
Sri Lanka beat England by 10 wickets
- New Zealand beat South Africa by 49 runs

Semi-Final Results:
- India beat Pakistan by 29 runs (Mohali)
- Sri Lanka beat New Zealand by 5 wickets (Colombo)

=== KEY UNUSUAL EVENTS ===

- This was the first World Cup to use DRS (Decision Review System) in some matches
- Indian PM Manmohan Singh and Pakistan PM attended the semi-final together — "cricket diplomacy"
- Sachin Tendulkar's final World Cup — an emotional storyline throughout
- Yuvraj Singh played the tournament unknowingly with cancer
- The tournament was a financial and popular success, especially with India winning on home soil
- No player was given out "handled the ball" in the 2011 World Cup — this is a common misconception

ICC Cricket World Cup 2015 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

======================================================================

Tournament: 2015 ICC Cricket World Cup
Host Countries: Australia, New Zealand
Dates: 14 February – 29 March 2015
Teams: 14 (Afghanistan, Australia, Bangladesh, England, India, Ireland, New Zealand, Pakistan, Scotland, South Africa, Sri Lanka, United Arab Emirates, West Indies, Zimbabwe)
Winner: Australia (defeated New Zealand in the Final)
Runner-up: New Zealand
Player of the Tournament: Mitchell Starc
Top Run Scorer: Martin Guptill (547 runs)
Top Wicket Taker: Mitchell Starc (22 wickets, average 10.18, economy 3.50)

metadata.section_title: ICC Cricket World Cup 2015 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

chunks.text:
in the Final)
Runner-up: New Zealand
Player of the Tournament: Mitchell Starc
Top Run Scorer: Martin Guptill (547 runs)
Top Wicket Taker: Mitchell Starc (22 wickets, average 10.18, economy 3.50)

=== TOP MEMORABLE MOMENTS ===

1. The Greatest World Cup Knock? — Martin Guptill's 237* vs West Indies:
   Date: 21 March 2015. Venue: Wellington Regional Stadium, Wellington. Quarter-Final.
   Martin Guptill scored 237* off 163 balls — the highest individual score in World Cup history.
   He hit 24 fours and 11 sixes. New Zealand posted 393/6.
   West Indies were bowled out for 250. New Zealand won by 143 runs.
   This surpassed Gary Kirsten's 188* (1996) as the highest individual World Cup score.
   Guptill's innings remains THE highest score in any World Cup match to this day (as of 2023).

2. The 2015 Final — Australia 186/3 vs New Zealand 183 all out:
   Date: 29 March 2015. Venue: Melbourne Cricket Ground (MCG), Melbourne.
   New Zealand batted first and were bowled out for 183 in 45 overs.
   Grant Elliott (83 off 82 balls) was the top scorer for New Zealand.
   Mitchell Starc took 2/20 in 8 overs with the new ball — removing both openers cheaply.
   Australia chased it down comfortably, reaching 186/3 in 33.1 overs.
   Steve Smith scored 56* off 71 balls to guide Australia home.
   Player of the Match: James Faulkner (3/36).
   Australia won by 7 wickets, claiming their 5th World Cup title.
   Turning Point: Starc's opening spell that removed Brendon McCullum (0) and Williamson (12).

of the Match: James Faulkner (3/36).
   Australia won by 7 wickets, claiming their 5th World Cup title.
   Turning Point: Starc's opening spell that removed Brendon McCullum (0) and Williamson (12).

3. Mitchell Starc's Tournament Dominance:
   Mitchell Starc was named Player of the Tournament for his extraordinary bowling.
   22 wickets in 8 matches at an average of 10.18 and economy of 3.50.
   His ability to swing the ball at 150+ km/h made him virtually unplayable.
   His yorker to dismiss Brendon McCullum in the final was a tournament-defining delivery.

4. AB de Villiers' 162 off 66 balls vs West Indies:
   Date: 27 February 2015. Venue: Sydney Cricket Ground, Sydney.
   AB de Villiers smashed 162 off just 66 balls — the fastest 150 in ODI history at that time.
   He hit 17 fours and 8 sixes. South Africa posted 408/5.
   This was the highest individual score by a South African in World Cup history.
   South Africa won by 257 runs — one of the largest margins in WC history.

and 8 sixes. South Africa posted 408/5.
   This was the highest individual score by a South African in World Cup history.
   South Africa won by 257 runs — one of the largest margins in WC history.

5. South Africa's Semi-Final Heartbreak (Again) — Grant Elliott's Six:
   Date: 24 March 2015. Venue: Eden Park, Auckland.
   South Africa vs New Zealand semi-final — one of the greatest World Cup matches ever.
   South Africa posted 281/5. Dale Steyn and Morne Morkel gave everything with the ball.
   New Zealand needed 298 (DLS revised) and were struggling at 149/4.
   Grant Elliott hit a six off Dale Steyn's penultimate over to win the match for New Zealand.
   Elliott's match-winning 84* off 73 balls ended South Africa's World Cup dream once more.
   Dale Steyn was in tears. The image of Elliott consoling Steyn became one of the most poignant World Cup photographs.
   South Africa's "knockout chokers" narrative reached its most painful chapter.

Steyn was in tears. The image of Elliott consoling Steyn became one of the most poignant World Cup photographs.
   South Africa's "knockout chokers" narrative reached its most painful chapter.

6. Brendon McCullum's Explosive Batting:
   New Zealand captain Brendon McCullum was the tournament's most exciting batsman.
   He scored a century off just 77 balls vs England — setting the tone for NZ's campaign.
   McCullum's ultra-aggressive approach, especially in the powerplay, changed how teams thought about ODI batting.

7. Ireland Nearly Stun Australia:
   In a group match at WACA Perth, Ireland came agonizingly close to beating Australia.
   Ireland scored 304. Australia chased it down in the final over, winning by 2 wickets.

8. England's Dismal Performance:
   England had a terrible tournament, losing to Bangladesh, New Zealand, Sri Lanka, and Australia.
   They were eliminated in the group stage — prompting a complete overhaul of their white-ball cricket philosophy.
   This humiliation led to the appointment of Trevor Bayliss as coach and the aggressive approach that eventually won them the 2019 World Cup.

overhaul of their white-ball cricket philosophy.
   This humiliation led to the appointment of Trevor Bayliss as coach and the aggressive approach that eventually won them the 2019 World Cup.

9. Afghanistan's First World Cup:
   Afghanistan played their first ever ODI World Cup match.
   They scored a historic win vs Scotland by 1 wicket in a thrilling match — their first-ever World Cup victory.
   Shapoor Zadran's winning shot celebrations went viral.

10. The Tournament of High Scores:
    The 2015 World Cup produced the most 300+ team totals in any World Cup.
    Flat pitches in Australia, combined with aggressive batting approaches, led to record-breaking scores.
    Chris Gayle scored 215 vs Zimbabwe — the first-ever double century in World Cup history.
    Gayle hit 10 fours and 16 sixes in his 215 off 147 balls. This was the highest score in 2015 WC before Guptill's 237*.

215 vs Zimbabwe — the first-ever double century in World Cup history.
    Gayle hit 10 fours and 16 sixes in his 215 off 147 balls. This was the highest score in 2015 WC before Guptill's 237*.

=== RECORDS SET IN 2015 WORLD CUP ===

- Martin Guptill 237* vs West Indies — highest individual score in World Cup history (still standing)
- Chris Gayle 215 vs Zimbabwe — first double century in World Cup history
- AB de Villiers 162 off 66 balls — fastest 150 in ODI history at that time
- Mitchell Starc — 22 wickets at average 10.18, Player of Tournament
- South Africa 408/5 vs West Indies — one of the highest team totals in WC
- Most 300+ team totals in any single World Cup tournament
- Australia's 5th World Cup title

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

1. Bangladesh beat England by 15 runs (Group A, 9 March 2015) — contributing to England's elimination

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

1. Bangladesh beat England by 15 runs (Group A, 9 March 2015) — contributing to England's elimination

2. Afghanistan beat Scotland by 1 wicket (Group A, 26 Feb 2015) — Afghanistan's first-ever WC win

3. Ireland almost beat Australia (lost by 2 wickets)

4. Sri Lanka beat England by 9 wickets (Group A)

=== TOURNAMENT FORMAT 2015 ===

Group Stage: 14 teams divided into 2 groups of 7 (Pool A and Pool B). Each team played every other team in their pool once. Top 4 from each pool advanced to the quarter-finals.
Quarter-Finals: 8 teams. A1 vs B4, A2 vs B3, A3 vs B2, A4 vs B1.
Semi-Finals: Winners of quarter-finals.
Final: Melbourne Cricket Ground, Melbourne on 29 March 2015.
Points: 2 for a win, 1 for tie/no-result, 0 for loss.

Pool A Qualified: New Zealand, Australia, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh
Pool B Qualified: India, South Africa, West Indies, Ireland (wait — actually Pakistan qualified from Pool B, not Ireland)

- New Zealand beat South Africa by 4 wickets (Auckland) — Grant Elliott's six

=== KEY UNUSUAL EVENTS ===

- Chris Gayle scored the first-ever World Cup double century (215 vs Zimbabwe)
- This was the last World Cup to feature quarter-finals — the format was changed for 2019
- England's dismal performance led to a complete white-ball revolution
- The tournament showcased the impact of T20 batting styles on ODI cricket
- Spirit of Cricket award — Brendon McCullum was widely praised for New Zealand's sportsmanship throughout

Runner-up: New Zealand
Player of the Tournament: Kane Williamson (New Zealand)
Top Run Scorer: Rohit Sharma (648 runs, 5 centuries)
Top Wicket Taker: Mitchell Starc (27 wickets)

=== THE 2019 WORLD CUP FINAL — THE GREATEST CRICKET MATCH EVER PLAYED ===

Date: 14 July 2019
Venue: Lord's Cricket Ground, London, England

THIS IS THE MOST DRAMATIC CRICKET MATCH IN HISTORY. Here is exactly what happened:

FIRST INNINGS — New Zealand batted first:
New Zealand scored 241/8 in 50 overs.
Henry Nicholls top-scored with 55 off 77 balls.
Tom Latham scored 47.
New Zealand struggled against Chris Woakes (3/37) and Liam Plunkett (3/42).
241 seemed like a below-par total on a decent Lord's pitch.

SECOND INNINGS — England's chase:
England were overwhelming favorites but struggled badly.
England were 86/4 at one stage — in deep trouble.
Ben Stokes (84* off 98 balls) and Jos Buttler (59 off 60 balls) rebuilt the innings.
With 2 balls remaining, England needed 2 runs to win.
A throw from the outfield deflected off Ben Stokes' bat while he was running and went to the boundary — England were awarded 4 overthrows (6 runs total for that delivery).
This was a hugely controversial moment — some believed only 5 runs should have been awarded.
England finished their 50 overs on exactly 241 — SCORES WERE TIED.

(6 runs total for that delivery).
This was a hugely controversial moment — some believed only 5 runs should have been awarded.
England finished their 50 overs on exactly 241 — SCORES WERE TIED.

THE SUPER OVER:
Because the match was tied after 50 overs each, a Super Over was required.
This was the FIRST EVER Super Over in a World Cup final.

England batted first in the Super Over:
Ben Stokes and Jos Buttler faced Trent Boult.
England scored 15 runs off 6 balls.

New Zealand batted second in the Super Over:
Martin Guptill and Jimmy Neesham faced Jofra Archer.
Jimmy Neesham hit a massive six off the second ball — New Zealand needed 2 off the last ball.
Martin Guptill hit the last ball toward mid-wicket and ran. Jason Roy's throw came to Jos Buttler.
Guptill was RUN OUT attempting the second run. New Zealand scored 15 off their Super Over.

ball.
Martin Guptill hit the last ball toward mid-wicket and ran. Jason Roy's throw came to Jos Buttler.
Guptill was RUN OUT attempting the second run. New Zealand scored 15 off their Super Over.

THE SUPER OVER WAS ALSO TIED — 15 runs each.

BOUNDARY COUNTBACK RULE:
Because the Super Over was also tied, the winner was decided by the BOUNDARY COUNTBACK RULE.
This rule stated: "In the event of a tied Super Over, the team that hit more boundaries in the match (including the Super Over) wins."

England hit 26 boundaries in the match (including Super Over): 22 fours + 2 sixes in regulation, plus 2 boundaries in Super Over.
New Zealand hit 17 boundaries in the match (including Super Over): 14 fours + 2 sixes in regulation, plus 1 six in Super Over.

ENGLAND WON THE 2019 WORLD CUP BY BOUNDARY COUNTBACK.
England won their FIRST EVER Cricket World Cup.

This boundary countback rule was widely criticized as unfair. The ICC later ABOLISHED the boundary countback rule.
From 2019 onwards, if a Super Over is tied, another Super Over would be played until there is a winner.

Player of the Match: Ben Stokes.

KEY CONTROVERSY: The overthrow incident — Stokes' bat deflection:
With England needing 9 off 3 balls, Stokes hit the ball and ran. The fielder's throw hit Stokes' bat as he dived to make his ground, and the ball deflected to the boundary.
The umpires awarded 6 runs (the 2 already run + 4 for the overthrow boundary).
Under Law 19.8, some experts argued only 5 runs should have been given (1 run completed + 4 for the overthrow), as the batsmen had not crossed at the moment the throw was made.
This remains one of the most debated umpiring decisions in cricket history.

have been given (1 run completed + 4 for the overthrow), as the batsmen had not crossed at the moment the throw was made.
This remains one of the most debated umpiring decisions in cricket history.

=== TOP MEMORABLE MOMENTS (Beyond the Final) ===

1. Rohit Sharma's Record 5 Centuries:
   Rohit Sharma scored FIVE centuries in a single World Cup — a record that still stands.
   His centuries: 122* vs South Africa, 140 vs Pakistan, 104 vs Bangladesh, 102 vs England, 103 vs Sri Lanka.
   Total runs: 648 runs in 9 innings at average 81.00.
   He broke Kumar Sangakkara's record of 4 centuries in a single World Cup (set in 2015).

2. David Warner's Resurgence:
   David Warner returned from a 12-month ban for ball-tampering.
   He scored 647 runs — second only to Rohit Sharma.
   His 166 vs Bangladesh was the highest individual score of the tournament.

David Warner returned from a 12-month ban for ball-tampering.
   He scored 647 runs — second only to Rohit Sharma.
   His 166 vs Bangladesh was the highest individual score of the tournament.

3. Shakib Al Hasan's All-Round Masterclass:
   Shakib scored 606 runs and took 11 wickets — the best all-round performance in World Cup history.
   He was Bangladesh's best player by a massive margin.

4. Carlos Brathwaite's Near-Miracle vs New Zealand:
   West Indies needed 6 off the last ball vs New Zealand.
   Carlos Brathwaite hit a massive six... but Trent Boult caught him on the boundary!
   Boult's foot touched the boundary rope — but he threw the ball up before stepping back in to complete the catch.
   West Indies lost by 5 runs. One of the most dramatic finishes in WC history.

5. Pakistan's Roller-Coaster Campaign:
   Pakistan lost their first match to West Indies, then beat England (the hosts).
   They remained in contention until the final group matches but were eliminated due to net run rate.
   Babar Azam scored 474 runs — his breakthrough tournament.

then beat England (the hosts).
   They remained in contention until the final group matches but were eliminated due to net run rate.
   Babar Azam scored 474 runs — his breakthrough tournament.

6. Mitchell Starc's Record 27 Wickets:
   Mitchell Starc broke Glenn McGrath's all-time World Cup record of 26 wickets in a single tournament.
   Starc took 27 wickets in 10 matches at average 18.59 — the most by any bowler in a single World Cup.
   Glenn McGrath's record (26 in 2007) had stood for 12 years.

7. India's Dominant Campaign Ends in Semi-Final:
   India won 7 of 9 group matches and finished top of the table.
   In the semi-final vs New Zealand (spread over 2 days due to rain), India lost by 18 runs.
   Jadeja (77) and Dhoni (50) nearly pulled off a miracle chase.
   MS Dhoni was run out by Martin Guptill's direct hit — one of the most emotional moments.
   This was Dhoni's last international innings. He never played for India again.

off a miracle chase.
   MS Dhoni was run out by Martin Guptill's direct hit — one of the most emotional moments.
   This was Dhoni's last international innings. He never played for India again.

8. Jason Roy and Jonny Bairstow — England's Opening Dominance:
   Roy (443 runs) and Bairstow (532 runs) were the most destructive opening pair.
   They set the platform for England's aggressive approach throughout the tournament.

9. Afghanistan's Rashid Khan Struggle:
   In one of the most difficult days for any bowler, Rashid Khan conceded 110 runs off 9 overs vs England.
   These are the most expensive bowling figures in World Cup history.

10. South Africa's Disappointing Campaign:
    South Africa had a terrible tournament, winning only 3 of 9 matches.
    They were eliminated in the group stage for the first time since 2003.

South Africa's Disappointing Campaign:
    South Africa had a terrible tournament, winning only 3 of 9 matches.
    They were eliminated in the group stage for the first time since 2003.

=== RECORDS SET IN 2019 WORLD CUP ===

- Rohit Sharma: 5 centuries in a single World Cup (record)
- Mitchell Starc: 27 wickets in a single World Cup (record, breaking McGrath's 26)
- First Super Over in a World Cup final
- First World Cup final decided by boundary countback
- Boundary countback rule subsequently abolished by ICC
- Shakib Al Hasan: 606 runs + 11 wickets (best all-round WC performance)
- Rashid Khan: 0/110 off 9 overs vs England (most expensive WC bowling figures)
- England won their first-ever World Cup
- David Warner: 647 runs (second highest in a single WC)

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

Rashid Khan: 0/110 off 9 overs vs England (most expensive WC bowling figures)
- England won their first-ever World Cup
- David Warner: 647 runs (second highest in a single WC)

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

1. Pakistan beat England by 14 runs (3 June 2019, Trent Bridge) — hosts beaten

2. Sri Lanka beat England by 20 runs (21 June 2019, Headingley)

3. West Indies beat Pakistan by 7 wickets (31 May 2019, opening match upset)

4. Bangladesh beat South Africa by 21 runs (2 June 2019, The Oval)

=== TOURNAMENT FORMAT 2019 ===

This World Cup used a ROUND-ROBIN format — completely different from previous World Cups.
- Only 10 teams participated (reduced from 14 in 2015).
- Every team played every other team ONCE — 45 group matches total.
- The top 4 teams in the standings qualified directly for the semi-finals.
- There were NO quarter-finals.
- Semi-Final 1 (1st vs 4th) and Semi-Final 2 (2nd vs 3rd).
- Points: 2 for a win, 1 for tie/no-result, 0 for loss.
- Tiebreaker: Net Run Rate (NRR).

Final Standings (Top 4 qualified):

=== KEY UNUSUAL EVENTS ===

- The boundary countback rule was used for the first and last time in cricket history
- The overthrow off Stokes' bat remains the most controversial moment in WC final history
- Dhoni's run out in the semi-final was his last ever international cricket moment
- Pakistan and New Zealand both had 11 points but Pakistan were eliminated on NRR — the tightest NRR elimination ever
- Rain caused the India vs New Zealand semi-final to be played over 2 days — first time a WC semi-final was a 2-day match
- This was the first 10-team World Cup since 1992 (round-robin format revival)
- Eoin Morgan hit 17 sixes vs Afghanistan — world record for most sixes in an ODI innings
- Controversy over England's 'new approach' to white-ball cricket paying off after their 2015 disaster

Eoin Morgan hit 17 sixes vs Afghanistan — world record for most sixes in an ODI innings
- Controversy over England's 'new approach' to white-ball cricket paying off after their 2015 disaster

=== IMPORTANT CLARIFICATIONS (to prevent common misconceptions) ===

1. The 2019 final was NOT decided by wickets — it was decided by BOUNDARY COUNTBACK after both the match AND Super Over were tied.

2. Rohit Sharma did NOT score a double century (200+) in the 2019 World Cup. His highest score was 140 vs Pakistan. His ODI double centuries (209, 264, 208*) were all scored in bilateral series, NOT in World Cups.

3. Ben Stokes was NOT out when the overthrow hit his bat — he was running between the wickets and the ball deflected accidentally.

4. The Super Over rule was: each team faces 6 balls, team with more runs wins. If tied, boundary countback (now abolished — would be repeated Super Overs).

5. Kane Williamson won Player of the Tournament despite New Zealand losing the final. He scored 578 runs at average 82.57.

ICC Cricket World Cup 2023 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

======================================================================

Tournament: 2023 ICC Cricket World Cup
Host Country: India
Dates: 5 October – 19 November 2023
Teams: 10 (Afghanistan, Australia, Bangladesh, England, India, Netherlands, New Zealand, Pakistan, South Africa, Sri Lanka)
Winner: Australia (defeated India in the Final)
Runner-up: India
Player of the Tournament: Virat Kohli (India)
Top Run Scorer: Virat Kohli (765 runs, 3 centuries)
Top Wicket Taker: Mohammed Shami (24 wickets in just 7 matches)

=== THE 2023 WORLD CUP FINAL ===

Date: 19 November 2023
Venue: Narendra Modi Stadium, Ahmedabad, India (capacity: 132,000)

metadata.section_title: ICC Cricket World Cup 2023 — Memorable Moments, Records & Key Events

chunks.text:
Wicket Taker: Mohammed Shami (24 wickets in just 7 matches)

=== THE 2023 WORLD CUP FINAL ===

Date: 19 November 2023
Venue: Narendra Modi Stadium, Ahmedabad, India (capacity: 132,000)

FIRST INNINGS — India batted first:
India scored 240 all out in 50 overs — a disappointing total given their dominance all tournament.
Virat Kohli scored 54 (his 50th ODI century came earlier in the tournament, not in the final).
KL Rahul scored 66 off 107 balls.
Pat Cummins took 2/34 and Mitchell Starc took 3/55.
Australia's bowling was disciplined — they restricted India on a surface that offered some assistance.

SECOND INNINGS — Australia's chase:
Travis Head scored a stunning 137 off 120 balls — one of the greatest World Cup final knocks.
Head was dropped by KL Rahul early in his innings — a costly miss that changed the match.
Marnus Labuschagne scored 58 off 110 balls, providing a crucial partnership with Head.
Australia reached 241/4 in 43 overs — winning by 6 wickets.
Player of the Match: Travis Head.

Turning Point: KL Rahul's dropped catch of Travis Head when Head was on 6. Head went on to score 137.
This was India's FIRST home World Cup final loss and ended their 10-match winning streak in the tournament.
Over 130,000 fans in the stadium fell silent as Australia celebrated.

Australia won their RECORD 6th World Cup title (1987, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2015, 2023).
They are the most successful team in World Cup history.

metadata.section_title: Wicket Taker: Mohammed Shami (24 wickets in just 7 matches)

chunks.text:
fans in the stadium fell silent as Australia celebrated.

Australia won their RECORD 6th World Cup title (1987, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2015, 2023).
They are the most successful team in World Cup history.

=== TOP MEMORABLE MOMENTS ===

1. India's Perfect 10 — 10 Consecutive Wins Then Final Loss:
   India won ALL 10 matches before the final — a record unbeaten streak in a single World Cup.
   They dominated every opponent in the group stage and semi-final.
   But they lost the one match that mattered most — the final.
   This was heartbreaking for 1.4 billion Indian fans and the 130,000+ in attendance.

2. Virat Kohli's Record 50th ODI Century:
   Date: 15 November 2023. Match: India vs South Africa, Semi-Final, Kolkata.
   Kohli scored 101* to guide India to the final.
   This was his 50th ODI century — breaking Sachin Tendulkar's all-time record of 49 ODI centuries.
   Tendulkar himself was present at Eden Gardens to witness the moment.
   One of the most emotional moments in cricket history — Kohli touched Tendulkar's feet in the stands.
   Kohli finished the tournament with 765 runs — the most by any batter in 2023 WC.

moment.
   One of the most emotional moments in cricket history — Kohli touched Tendulkar's feet in the stands.
   Kohli finished the tournament with 765 runs — the most by any batter in 2023 WC.

3. Mohammed Shami's Extraordinary Campaign:
   Shami took 24 wickets in just 7 matches at an average of 10.70.
   He was not in India's original XI — he came in as a replacement after the first match.
   Shami took 7/57 vs New Zealand in the semi-final — the best figures by an Indian in World Cups.
   His 5-wicket hauls: 5/54 vs Sri Lanka, 5/18 vs England (yes, FIVE for EIGHTEEN).
   He had the best strike rate (10.7) and average (10.70) of any bowler in the tournament.

4. Travis Head's Final Masterclass — 137 off 120:
   Head's century in the final was arguably the greatest pressure knock in recent WC history.
   He was dropped on 6 by KL Rahul — and made India pay the ultimate price.
   His 137 included 15 fours and 4 sixes.
   Head had been in excellent form but this innings was on another level.

He was dropped on 6 by KL Rahul — and made India pay the ultimate price.
   His 137 included 15 fours and 4 sixes.
   Head had been in excellent form but this innings was on another level.

5. Afghanistan's Historic Semi-Final Dream:
   Afghanistan had their best-ever World Cup, beating England, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka.
   They came close to qualifying for the semi-finals for the first time.
   Rahmanullah Gurbaz (568 runs) and Ibrahim Zadran were outstanding.
   Their victory over defending champions England (69-run win) was historic.

6. England's Title Defense Disaster:
   Defending champions England had one of the worst title defenses ever.
   They lost to New Zealand, Afghanistan, India, South Africa, Sri Lanka, and Australia.
   They won only 3 of 9 group matches and were eliminated in the group stage.
   Jos Buttler's captaincy was heavily criticized.

Zealand, Afghanistan, India, South Africa, Sri Lanka, and Australia.
   They won only 3 of 9 group matches and were eliminated in the group stage.
   Jos Buttler's captaincy was heavily criticized.

7. Netherlands' Heroics:
   Netherlands, the only Associate team, punched above their weight.
   They beat South Africa by 38 runs — one of the biggest upsets of the tournament.

8. South Africa's Semi-Final Heartbreak (Again):
   South Africa qualified for the semi-final but lost to Australia by 3 wickets.
   Travis Head (52) and David Warner were the key contributors.
   South Africa's "chokers" label in knockout matches continued.

9. Jasprit Bumrah's Return:
   Bumrah returned from a back injury and took 20 wickets at average 18.65.
   His economy of 4.06 was exceptional on Indian pitches.
   He and Shami formed the most lethal pace pair in the tournament.

returned from a back injury and took 20 wickets at average 18.65.
   His economy of 4.06 was exceptional on Indian pitches.
   He and Shami formed the most lethal pace pair in the tournament.

10. The Greatest ODI Team... That Lost the Final:
    India 2023 is statistically one of the greatest ODI teams ever assembled.
    They scored the most runs, took the most wickets, had the best NRR.
    Rohit Sharma's aggressive captaincy — including hitting 31 sixes in the tournament (personal record).
    Shubman Gill, Shreyas Iyer, and KL Rahul all contributed heavily.
    But they lost the one match that defined everything — the final.

=== RECORDS SET IN 2023 WORLD CUP ===

- Virat Kohli: 50th ODI century (all-time record, surpassing Tendulkar's 49)
- Virat Kohli: 765 runs — highest by any batter in 2023 WC
- Mohammed Shami: 24 wickets in 7 matches (best strike rate in any WC)
- India: Won 10 consecutive matches in a single World Cup (record)
- Australia: 6th World Cup title (most by any team ever)
- Travis Head: 137 in the final — highest score in a WC final chase
- Mitchell Starc: Extended his record as highest WC wicket-taker with career total
- Rohit Sharma: Most sixes in a single World Cup (31)
- Largest stadium to host a WC final — Narendra Modi Stadium (132,000 capacity)
- Quinton de Kock: 3 centuries in the tournament

=== MAJOR UPSETS ===

1. Afghanistan beat England by 69 runs (15 Oct, Delhi) — defending champions humiliated

2. Netherlands beat South Africa by 38 runs (17 Oct, Dharamsala)

3. Afghanistan beat Pakistan by 8 wickets (23 Oct, Chennai)

4. Sri Lanka beat England by 8 wickets (20 Oct, Bengaluru)

=== TOURNAMENT FORMAT 2023 ===

This World Cup used the SAME round-robin format as 2019:
- 10 teams, every team plays every other team once — 45 group matches.
- Top 4 qualify for semi-finals directly.
- NO quarter-finals.
- Semi-Final 1 (1st vs 4th) and Semi-Final 2 (2nd vs 3rd).
- Points: 2 for win, 1 for tie/NR, 0 for loss.

Final Standings:

Correction on Kohli's 50th century: It was scored on 15 November vs New Zealand in the semi-final at Mumbai (not vs South Africa). He scored 117 off 121 balls in that semi-final.

Wait — let me clarify: Kohli's 49th century equalled Tendulkar's record vs South Africa on 2 November at Kolkata. His 50th ODI century (breaking the record) came on 15 November vs New Zealand in the semi-final at Mumbai's Wankhede Stadium, where he scored 117.

Final: India vs Australia at Ahmedabad — Australia won by 6 wickets.

=== KEY UNUSUAL EVENTS ===

- The Narendra Modi Stadium in Ahmedabad (132,000 capacity) hosted the final — the largest cricket ground in the world
- India played ALL their group matches and semi-final at home — leading to debate about home advantage
- Dew factor played a huge role in evening matches — teams batting second had an advantage in many games
- This was potentially Virat Kohli's last ODI World Cup (aged 35 in 2023)
- The tournament saw a return of genuine fast bowling dominance — Starc, Bumrah, Shami, Shaheen, Boult
- Australia have now won the World Cup more times (6) than any other team
- Pat Cummins captained Australia to the World Cup title while also being their frontline fast bowler

=== IMPORTANT CLARIFICATIONS ===

1. India did NOT win 11 matches — they won 10 (9 group + 1 semi-final) and lost the final

2. Kohli's 50th century was in the semi-final vs New Zealand at Mumbai, scoring 117 off 121 balls

3. Mohammed Shami played only 7 matches (not all 11) because he was NOT in the starting XI initially

4. Australia had LOST to India in the group stage but beat them in the final

5. The 2023 format was round-robin (same as 2019) — there were NO quarter-finals

6. Travis Head's 137 was in a chase of 241 — so it was scored under pressure but on a gettable target

ICC Cricket World Cup — All-Time Records, Cross-Tournament Facts & Key Statistics

==================================================================================

=== WORLD CUP WINNERS — COMPLETE LIST ===

1975: West Indies (beat Australia by 17 runs, Lord's, London)
1979: West Indies (beat England by 92 runs, Lord's, London)
1983: India (beat West Indies by 43 runs, Lord's, London) — Kapil Dev captain
1987: Australia (beat England by 7 runs, Eden Gardens, Kolkata) — Allan Border captain
1992: Pakistan (beat England by 22 runs, MCG, Melbourne) — Imran Khan captain
1996: Sri Lanka (beat Australia by 7 wickets, Gaddafi Stadium, Lahore) — Arjuna Ranatunga captain
1999: Australia (beat Pakistan by 8 wickets, Lord's, London) — Steve Waugh captain
2003: Australia (beat India by 125 runs, Wanderers, Johannesburg) — Ricky Ponting captain
2007: Australia (beat Sri Lanka by 53 runs (DLS), Kensington Oval, Barbados) — Ricky Ponting captain
2011: India (beat Sri Lanka by 6 wickets, Wankhede Stadium, Mumbai) — MS Dhoni captain
2015: Australia (beat New Zealand by 7 wickets, MCG, Melbourne) — Michael Clarke captain
2019: England (beat New Zealand via Super Over + boundary countback, Lord's, London) — Eoin Morgan captain
2023: Australia (beat India by 6 wickets, Narendra Modi Stadium, Ahmedabad) — Pat Cummins captain

=== WORLD CUP TITLES BY TEAM ===

India: 2 titles (1983, 2011)
England: 1 title (2019)
Pakistan: 1 title (1992)
Sri Lanka: 1 title (1996)

=== WORLD CUP FORMAT EVOLUTION ===

1975-1979: 8 teams. Round-robin groups, semi-finals, final. 60 overs per side.
1983: 8 teams. Round-robin groups (played twice), semi-finals, final. 60 overs.
1987: 8 teams. First WC outside England. Reduced to 50 overs per side.
1992: 9 teams. Round-robin (every team plays every other), semi-finals, final. Colored clothing, white ball, day-night matches introduced.
1996: 12 teams. Groups + quarter-finals + semi-finals + final.
1999: 12 teams. Groups + Super Six + semi-finals + final.
2003: 14 teams. Groups + Super Six + semi-finals + final.
2007: 16 teams. Groups + Super Eight + semi-finals + final.
2011: 14 teams. Groups + quarter-finals + semi-finals + final.
2015: 14 teams. Pools + quarter-finals + semi-finals + final. LAST WC with quarter-finals.
2019: 10 teams. Round-robin (every team plays every other) + semi-finals + final. NO quarter-finals.
2023: 10 teams. Same round-robin format as 2019.

2023: 10 teams. Same round-robin format as 2019.

=== SUPER OVERS IN WORLD CUP ===

There has been ONLY ONE Super Over in World Cup history — the 2019 Final.
England vs New Zealand: Match tied at 241. Super Over tied at 15-15. England won via boundary countback.
The boundary countback rule was abolished after the 2019 World Cup.

=== COMMON QUIZ FACTS ===

- Only player to score a century in a World Cup final: Adam Gilchrist (149 in 2007), Ricky Ponting (140* in 2003), Jayawardene (103* in 2011), Travis Head (137 in 2023), Clive Lloyd (102 in 1975)
- Most World Cup final appearances: Australia (8 finals — 1975, 1987, 1996, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2015, 2023)
- Most consecutive WC titles: Australia 3 (1999, 2003, 2007)
- First Asian team to win WC: India (1983)
- Youngest WC centurion: Various claims, Tendulkar was very young
- Only team to win WC as host: India in 2011, Australia in 2015
- Sachin Tendulkar: Played 6 World Cups (1992, 1996, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2011) — most by any player
- Most World Cup matches: Sachin Tendulkar (45 matches)

WC as host: India in 2011, Australia in 2015
- Sachin Tendulkar: Played 6 World Cups (1992, 1996, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2011) — most by any player
- Most World Cup matches: Sachin Tendulkar (45 matches)

=== RICKY PONTING'S WORLD CUP RECORD ===

Ponting played 5 World Cups (1996, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2011).
World Cup runs: 1,743 in 46 matches.
Centuries: He scored 140* in the 2003 final vs India (Player of the Match).
In the 2007 final vs Sri Lanka, Ponting captained Australia to victory. He scored 37 in that final (not a big score, but Australia won comfortably).
He was captain for Australia's 3 consecutive WC wins (2003, 2007).
In 2011, Ponting's last WC, Australia were eliminated in the quarter-final by India.

=== VIRAT KOHLI'S WORLD CUP RECORD ===

Kohli played 4 World Cups (2011, 2015, 2019, 2023).
2011: Scored 282 runs (avg 35.25), including 100* vs Bangladesh. WON the World Cup.
2015: Scored 305 runs (avg 50.83), 1 century. India lost in semi-final to Australia.
2019: Scored 443 runs (avg 55.37), India lost in semi-final to New Zealand.
2023: Scored 765 runs (avg 95.62), 3 centuries. India lost in final to Australia. Player of Tournament.

Kohli's World Cup knockout record (semi-finals and finals only):
2011 final: Scored 35 (India won)
2015 semi vs Australia: Scored 1 (India lost)
2019 semi vs New Zealand: Scored 1 (India lost)
2023 final vs Australia: Scored 54 (India lost)

=== AB DE VILLIERS' WORLD CUP RECORD ===

AB de Villiers played 3 World Cups (2007, 2011, 2015).
2007: South Africa eliminated in Super Eights. De Villiers scored some runs but was not dominant.
2011: South Africa eliminated in quarter-final by New Zealand. De Villiers scored 353 runs.
2015: De Villiers' best WC. He scored 482 runs at avg 96.40, including 162* off 66 balls vs WI.
South Africa lost the semi-final to New Zealand (Grant Elliott's six).
De Villiers NEVER scored the same score in all 3 World Cups. His scores varied match to match.

=== CHRIS GAYLE'S WORLD CUP SIXES ===

Chris Gayle played 5 World Cups (2003, 2007, 2011, 2015, 2019).
He is one of the biggest six-hitters in World Cup history.
His 215 vs Zimbabwe in 2015 included 16 sixes — the most in a single WC innings.
Career WC sixes: Gayle hit a significant number across his 5 tournaments.
His most prolific WC was 2015 where he scored 340 runs with many sixes.

=== SACHIN TENDULKAR'S WORLD CUP RECORD ===

Tendulkar played 6 World Cups (1992, 1996, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2011).
Total: 2,278 runs in 45 matches at average 56.95 — MOST runs in WC history.
Centuries: 6 centuries in World Cups.
Best WC: 2003 — 673 runs including 98 vs Pakistan (iconic innings at Centurion).
2011: Tendulkar's last WC. India won. He scored 482 runs. Carried off the field by teammates.
Tendulkar holds the record for most 50+ scores in World Cups.

=== MS DHONI'S WORLD CUP RECORD ===

Dhoni played 4 World Cups (2007, 2011, 2015, 2019).
2007: Young Dhoni was part of the squad but India was eliminated in group stage.
2011: Captain. Won the World Cup. Hit the winning six (a massive six over long-on off Nuwan Kulasekara). Scored 91* in the final.
2015: Captain. India lost in semi-final to Australia. Dhoni scored 65 in that semi.
2019: India lost in semi-final to New Zealand. Dhoni scored 50 before being run out by Guptill's direct hit. This was his LAST international innings. He retired from international cricket on 15 August 2020.
